articles.title: Punk Rocker Birds Win a Big Photo Contest!
articles.summary: Three baby egrets with spiky feathers that look like tiny mohawks won the grand prize in Smithsonian Magazine's 23rd annual photography contest. The photo, called "The Punk Rockers," shows the chicks squabbling and playing under a ramp in New Jersey. A 64-year-old veterinarian took the picture. Over 17,000 photos from 108 countries were entered. Other winners include a woman in a black veil, a Hindu festival scene, a heavy Easter procession in Sicily, a Navajo girl's coming-of-age ceremony, a colorful chameleon, a circular building in China, and a Mundari child cleaning a bull's horns.

articles.title: Weaving Women: How a Group Helps Moms in Peru Earn Money and Keep Traditions Alive
articles.summary: In Peru's Sacred Valley, Quechua women have been weaving beautiful textiles for centuries. But as the world changes, they need money for things like school supplies and medicine. A group called Awamaki helps them sell their weavings and earn their own income. Today, 174 women in nine cooperatives work with Awamaki. They raise alpacas, shear their wool, spin it into yarn, and weave it into blankets and clothes. The women say having their own money lets them make decisions together with their families. It also helps them keep their traditions alive, even as the climate changes and glaciers melt.

articles.title: Rocky's Statue Gets Its Own Art Show!
articles.summary: You know the Rocky statue at the Philadelphia Museum of Art? The one that millions of people visit and take photos with? Now there's a whole art exhibition about it! It's called "Rising Up: Rocky and the Making of Monuments." The show has over 150 works by more than 50 artists. It includes ancient sculptures, boxing photos, and modern art by famous artists like Andy Warhol and Jean-Michel Basquiat. The exhibition explores why the Rocky statue—a movie prop that became a real monument—means so much to people. It's about the underdog spirit: perseverance, grit, and hope.
